title: Cross-Merchant BIN Sweep Testing for Weak Authorization Controls
narrative: Fraudsters systematically test a sequential BIN range (e.g., 423456XXXXXX through 423456XX9999) across 15-30 different merchants within a 90-minute window to identify which merchant IDs have weak authorization controls or lax fraud filters. Each card number is tested once per merchant with small authorization probes ($1.00-$4.99 TransactionAmt values). Attackers use a single P_emaildomain (often disposable services like guerrillamail or temp-mail) across all test transactions, with identical addr1 and dist1 values despite varying merchant MCCs. The pattern creates a distinctive signature: card1 values increment sequentially within the same BIN range, each appearing at 12-25 unique MIDs during late-night hours (txn_hour 23-04), all declining or approving within seconds. Successful authorizations at specific merchants then trigger high-value fraud attempts within 6-12 hours using validated card numbers.
indicators: Sequential card1 values within same 8-digit BIN tested across 15+ distinct MIDs in under 120 minutes
All transactions have TransactionAmt between $1.00-$4.99 with identical P_emaildomain across different merchants
85%+ of transactions occur during is_night=1 window (txn_hour between 23-05)
card1_txn_count_MID equals 1 while card1_txn_count_global reaches 18-30 within 2-hour span
Identical addr1 and dist1 values used across merchants with varying MCCs (5411, 5732, 5999, 7999)
card1_amt_zscore remains flat near 0 during testing phase, then spikes to 3+ within 12 hours for validated cards
No CVV match variation across merchants (consistent CVV2 pass or fail per sequential card number)
summary: Sequential card numbers from the same BIN are tested once at 15-30 different merchants within 90 minutes, all using tiny amounts ($1-5), the same email domain, and identical billing details despite different merchant types. The testing happens late at night, and each card hits many merchants but only once per merchant.
reasoning: Attackers are probing to find which merchants have weak fraud filters before committing larger fraud. The sequential card generation, cross-merchant spread, and disposable email reveal they're systematically mapping vulnerabilities rather than making legitimate purchases, then exploiting the weakest targets with validated card numbers.
indicator_explanations: Sequential card numbers from same bank are tested across many different merchants quickly
All test charges are tiny amounts using same throwaway email across unrelated stores
Nearly all activity happens during overnight hours when monitoring is weakest
Each card hits one merchant once but racks up attempts across many merchants
Same shipping address and location data reused across completely different merchant types
Test amounts stay normal then suddenly spike once attacker finds cards that work
CVV response is identical across merchants showing attacker has full card data
